sentences.text: Excuse me.
My name is Cissie Nathan.
I was born in London on the 8th of November, 1905.
Cissie Nathan?
My name is spelled C-I-S-S-I-E N-A-T-H-A-N.
My maiden name was Cissie Rabbie, R-A-B-B-I-E.
My dad was born in Holland.
His name was Moses Maurice Rabbie.
His profession, he was a agent, I believe, for a piano firm.
Oh, I was the eldest of five.
I'm-- my father was always very proud of me because I'd been to school and had a scholarship.
I'd been learning music, piano.
He bought me a piano.
And I won two certificates at the College of Music in London.
And-- but then my father, god rest his soul, died of it when influenza first came to England.
He was 38 years old.
He would've been 39 in January.
He died on my birthday-- birthday.
When he passed away, I was the eldest, naturally, and 14 on that day.
I had to sit shiva there we were with my mother.
After that, I had to give up school and had to try and earn some money to help my mother.
There was money left, but it wasn't sufficient.
There were five of us, and my grandmother lived with us in the house.
So I went to night school with a friend and learned shorthand and typing.
I got a position in an office in the city of London and used to travel up from where we lived every day back and forth.
My mother, god rest her soul, was a dressmaker by profession, so she took it up again.
But-- Tell us-- tell us your mother's name.
My mother's name was Rebecca Rabbie, of course.
So she took it-- the dressmaking up again.
But at that time they were wearing a lot of beading on their dresses, and she could not do that.
So when I came home from work, I used to bead the dresses till about 1 o'clock in the morning.
And then--
I was 14 on that day, yes, that my father died.
Yes.
Clark.
Rebecca Clark.
Yes.
Yes.
We were all very close family.
My grandmother, my mother's mother, lived with us.
We were all very-- she outlived my mother.
Pardon?
At 14.
Yeah.
Well, at that age, not really, you know.
As I got older, naturally, there was fun.
And I had girlfriends, and we used to go dancing when I was older.
And you know, we-- we had a close-knit family, so there was always something, because my dad's two brothers were married, lived in the same road where we lived, and all the children.
We were all very close, very happy together.
How many--
How many aunts?
Oh, I'm sorry.
I had two brothers and two sisters.
Yes.
One-- one sister is still alive.
She's in London in a home.
She has--
My sister who's still alive, her name is Lily.
Yeah.
My eldest brother's name was Sam, Samuel.
My other sister's name-- she was the one next to me-- her name was Anne.
And my youngest brother's name was Michael.
My sister that's still alive is in a home in London because she has Parkinson's disease.
I saw her this year.
I go and see her every time I'm over here, yeah.
Oh, we all grew up-- we were all very-- well, together.
We-- we were always close with all of them.
But it was a close-knit family with all the uncles and aunts and close-- my dad being the eldest.
You see, my dad's father died when he was young.
His mother-- so he came to England when he was 18, brought his mother with him.
And his two brothers came to England, too.
They were younger than my father.
The youngest brother, my Uncle Lou, had to go to school.
But the other brother was 14, and he went and did some work, which I don't remember what it was.
I think it was somewhere in a cigar factory, but that's all I remember.
In England.
I-- I was growing up in England until 1928.
My brother said to me, my eldest brother, would you like to go to the Olympics to see the swimming?
And I was always interested, so I said yes, I'll go with you.
So I went with him to Amsterdam to see the Olympic swimming.
And there I met my son's father.
His name was Isaac.
They called him [?
Ise. ?] And his name was Rabbie because he was a cousin.
Never met.
Never knew him.
He was strange to me.
Where?
The name of the--
London.
Yes.
I was born and bred in London.
We all were.
Yeah.
Right, for the Games.
And then, I think it was a couple of months later, he came over to England.
And we corresponded for quite a while.
And then he came over again and we got engaged.
And then-- that was, I think, 1929.
And in 1930, I went to Holland to get married.
My grandmother came with me at that time because I went in May and stayed with my grand-- had a son there.
Stayed there, and then my mother and the family came over.
We got married in August 1930.
I was 24 when that happened, yes.
We lived in Amsterdam.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
First I-- first I lived in the Jewish quarter where my in-laws lived.
And then we moved to the southern quarter of Amsterdam, a whole new area.
Yeah.
I-- and my husband being a diamond worker, there was a recession then.
The recession started.
So I went to work in a office in Amsterdam, English, doing English correspondence.
After that, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I became pregnant and left work.
And Maurice, my son was born, Maurice.
My son was born on the 15th of March, 1932.
Yes.
Not strict Orthodox, but they were religious.
We kept everything. yeah.
Or [INAUDIBLE] my grandmother was religious.
Usually, not always, but usually the brothers used to come with their family.
If any relatives from Holland came over, it was always in our house that all the family came, all the family.
I remember the First World War, '14-'18 war.
When there was an air raid, a lot of people used to go underground or to the station.
But my dad-- we had a very large dining room table-- turned the table upside down and put all their little ones in it.
The men used to play cards.
The women used to knit, make the coffee, whatever.
I remember that vividly.
Oh, we had-- yes.
Everything was changed, everything.
Everything we did.
Everything that we were supposed to do.
My grandmother was religious.
Oh, here.
Every-- yes.
Oh, yes, sure.
And my son does the brochos.
He does it all.
[SOB]
Yeah.
No.
After my son and my daughter were born, I didn't work anymore.
No, I didn't have to work anymore.
But I ran the house well.
We had a-- they call it a condo here.
We had a flat, yes.
The life in Amsterdam was good.
I had a brother-in-law married with a daughter who went to Auschwitz.
My in-laws went to Auschwitz.
And I had a sister-in-law married with a little son who also went-- went to Auschwitz.
Well, we always-- we always used to see each other, always used to meet, always holidays or birthdays, always together.
And there was family of my father-in-law there, and we met up.
We used to meet up with all the family.
We had quite a good life there.
Really good life.
Yes.
And the food in Amsterdam is better than anywhere.
[LAUGHS]
There wasn't as far as we knew.
We never experienced it at all, not at all.
No, I didn't find that.
No, no, no.
And they all spoke English in Holland because they learn it at school.
So there was no problem.
No.
Where?
Yes.
Quite near where we lived.
It was Malmesbury Road School in Bow.
How much--
Oh, till I was 14.
I went to a primary school first.
And then at 11, you do the exams and you get a-- and I got a scholarship and then went on from that.
And I should have stayed on but I couldn't.
My mom had to come before the board and tell them why I had to leave, which I did, as I said before.
Yes.
They-- they-- yes.
They all continued school until they were old enough to go out to work.
Yes.
Oh, of course.
My mother remained with the other-- my brothers and sisters.
They were born-- all born in England.
Of course.
And my grandmother was there.
No, we all-- they all remained.
My-- when they were old enough, they went out to work the same as I did.
Well, it was very strange at first.
But we-- but one or the other always used to visit.
Yeah.
My mother used to come.
And I used to go to England with the children.
And then my late husband, my husband, Maurice's father, would come from a Friday till a Sunday, something like that, for weekends, when business permitted, of course, when work permitted.
Yeah.
Well, not really.
The only thing is, I wasn't very-- I didn't know a lot of hot Dutch.
My-- my late mother-in-law took me shopping, and you know, I learned more.
And then one day she said to me, now you're on your own.
Now you've got to do it yourself.
I had the two children then.
So there was the two children in the pram shopping.
We got on very well.
But after a while, when the children were born, I had a lady to help me once a week, you know, with the cleaning and that was my [INAUDIBLE].
My children started school, I think it was five.
Five years old.
They went to a Jewish kindergarten before that that I have some pictures of.
And just for a couple of hours in the morning, from 9:00 till 12:00, something like that.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Yeah.
Maybe not quite as strict.
But Jewish home, yeah.
My father and all used to light Hanukkah candles and yeah, yeah.
Oh, in a Jewish community, yes, yes, yes, yes.
Not at that time, no.
Not at all.
No, no.
Who?
Well, one girlfriend I met, Leah, she died three years ago.
She was in concentration camp too.
She was with me for a lot of the time.
I met Leah through a-- a lady cousin by marriage.
I met her, and then we were always very-- she worked after the war as a beautician.
And I was working as a millinery designer.
In Holland?
Nothing.
Only the family, and I-- I didn't work.
Only the period before the children were born.
Yes, she worked.
She was a beautician.
She worked, yes.
London friends?
No.
Never.
We only had Jewish friends.
Yeah.
But my mother was a sister of mercy.
That's what they used to call her.
The road we lived in was every religion, every religion.
And if somebody was sick, everybody helped, no matter what their religion was.
And you'd go to school with their children, you know.
They would-- I'll tell you a secret.
Sometimes on Simchat Torah, when they give children sweets, we used to take a couple of the others in with us sometimes.
Only two.
They were-- but my mom was a very good person.
Because nobody did at that time.
None of us did.
Only girls that I worked with in the office.
It was a big office, every nationality.
But not friends to go out with or anything.
No.
Not at all.
Because I had my home and my children.
Didn't have the time.
Oh, they were all right.
They were-- in the office we all got on very well together.
We had a very big office.
Yeah.
There was one German girl, non-Jewish.
She was very, very friendly.
Yeah.
She was very nice.
But I never went out with her, but just friendly in the office, you know.
We in Holland all thought that Holland would be neutral like it was in the First World War.
That's what everybody thought.
My mother, god rest her soul, came over in August 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the war started.
Came over to Holland, and she said, the boys-- that's my brothers-- want you all to come home because there's going to be trouble.
And my husband quite assured they wouldn't come through Holland.
But apart from that, so he-- he-- my mother said, if you won't come-- because I couldn't leave him-- if you won't come, let me take the children.
And Maurice's father said, where I go, my children go.
Wouldn't let them go.
So that's why we were caught in Amsterdam.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
My two brothers were in the British forces.
My eldest brother was a sick bay officer in the Royal Navy, and my youngest brother was in the Army.
My youngest brother was taken prisoner of war in Singapore.
He was in the Japanese prison camp.
But my eldest brother came home on compassionate leave when my mother was-- died.
My mother-- next to where we lived in England, in London, a schoolmaster lived.
And my mother had got a envelope-- telegram from the British forces to say that my brother was missing in action.
So that's why my eldest brother was home on compassionate leave.
Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we were in-- when we got transported from Amsterdam, we-- we were in one of the last groups to go to Westerbork, a camp in Holland.
I didn't know.
I know-- yeah, now.
I knew up to the war, when.
But that was later.
That was much later.
She died on the 4th of December, 1944, the same day and the same time that they took my husband, Maurice's father, when they separated us.
Yes.
We only realized there was a real danger when they-- the Germans went into Belgium and then came into Holland.
As a matter of fact, I was-- the day they entered Amsterdam, I was standing at the corner of the road where we lived with the doctor that birthed my two children and saw them march in.
You can imagine how I felt.
You can imagine how we all felt.
This doctor was a young Jewish doctor who went underground.
They changed his name and sent him in another hospital a long way away.
So he survived.
I saw him after the war.
I went back to where we lived, of course.
The children were there.
We lived.
And then my husband, being one of the diamond workers in Amsterdam, they were essential to the Germans because they needed the Germans to buy munitions.
So we had a special-- what did they call it?
A spare?
That if we were picked up, if I was picked up with the children, or my husband, they wouldn't take us.
